FT_IC1.evidence: Paper explicitly discusses the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, NIH-funded, with preliminary data from 3621 youth across sites and plans for future implementation at ABCD follow-up visits.

FT_IC5.evidence: Paper is a perspective/resource paper describing current, piloted, and future potential uses of mobile and wearable technologies in the ABCD study. It reports only feasibility pilot statistics (e.g., 88.5% found iPads easy to use) and proposes future data collection methods; it does not report an original inferential analysis of ABCD data examining associations between variables.

notes: Exclusion code FT-EC3 (non-empirical). Paper is a technology review and prospective planning document for ABCD data collection methods.

FT_IC1.evidence: Methods section explicitly states: "This study was embedded in the Amsterdam Born Children and their Development (ABCD) Study (www.abcd-study.nl), a community-based prospective cohort study" in the Netherlands (n=2354). This is the Dutch ABCD cohort, not the U.S. NIH-funded Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study.

notes: Exclusion code FT-EC1 (not U.S. ABCD). Cohort is the Amsterdam Born Children and their Development (Dutch ABCD) study from the Netherlands.

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper uses U.S. ABCD Study data release 5.0, N=9475, 21 sites, baseline ages 9-10, NIH-funded. Acknowledgments cite ABCD NDA Collection #2573 and multiple U01DA award numbers.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-sectional multivariable logistic regression examining sociodemographic predictors of early smartphone ownership age (<10 years) in ABCD participants.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Pediatrics, Volume 157, Issue 2, February 2026. No indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: The paper reports ORs for early smartphone ownership (<10 years old) as the DV in Table 2. The predictors are exclusively demographic variables: youth sex, race/ethnicity, household income (<$75,000 vs. ≥$75,000), parental marital status (single/living with partner/married), and parental education (<bachelor's vs. ≥bachelor's). Smartphone ownership qualifies as a phone/SM variable, but the other side of every analysis is demographic-only (sex, race/ethnicity, income, marital status, parental education). No individual-trait-level variable (behavioral, health, cognitive, mental health, etc.) appears on the other side of any analysis. FT-IC2 = NOT_MET under the other-side rule.

notes: Exclusion code FT-EC2. Paper examines sociodemographic correlates of early smartphone ownership age; the only predictors are demographic variables (sex, race/ethnicity, income, parental marital status, parental education). No individual-trait-level variable is paired with the smartphone variable in any analysis.
